text: This operation had some unexpected aberrant anatomy. I was performing this cholecystectomy for a one point one centimeter gallbladder polyp. I have routinely had the habit of dissecting the posterior wall of the gallbladder at least one third to one half of the way up the fossa before placing any clips on the duct or the artery. I always joke that this is out of paranoia, but in this case it proved to be an example where this extended dissection prevented a complication.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and cystic artery during the dissection. The cystic duct is seen inserting onto the infundibulum of the gallbladder. As the dissection progresses up the gallbladder fossa, another duct structure becomes visible, initially hinted at under firefly mode due to the preoperative administration of endocyanin green.

The surgeon continues dissection to isolate an aberrant duct structure, toggling between regular and firefly views to trace its course. A distinct plane is visible between the superior border of the structure and the gallbladder, with the firefly view aiding in the dissection.

The surgeon observes the course of an aberrant duct, noting its separation from the gallbladder and liver. The duct is seen inserting into the infundibulum of the gallbladder before joining the cystic duct.

The surgeon creates a window behind the gallbladder and above the aberrant duct, continuing dissection up the gallbladder fossa to check for additional anatomical aberrancies. The isolated structure is clearly delineated after careful dissection.

The surgeon reviews the anatomy using Firefly imaging, revealing an aberrant duct originating from the liver. The cystic duct is visible, and the common hepatic duct is located in its expected position. The Firefly mode highlights the aberrant duct's course, emphasizing the importance of careful dissection to avoid injury during gallbladder removal.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative cholangiogram to better understand the anatomy. A large clip is placed proximally on the apparent duct, and a 14 French angiocatheter is advanced into the abdominal cavity through the epigastrium. A ductotomy is performed on the infundibulum of the gallbladder, proximal to the insertion of the aberrant duct. The first cholangiogram is conducted on the cystic duct, with the balloon occluding the proximal system and the aberrant duct.

The surgeon positions a C-arm above the robotic arms to perform an intraoperative cholangiogram without undocking the robot. The first cholangiogram reveals the common bile duct, the left hepatic duct, and at least one right hepatic duct. The second cholangiogram is planned to isolate the aberrant duct.

The surgeon attempts to inflate the balloon but faces difficulty as the catheter dislodges from the aberrant duct. Despite this, the intraoperative cholangiogram reveals the significant branching of the aberrant duct. Distal clips are placed on the infundibulum to preserve the communication with the cystic duct.

This patient has done very well post-operatively. She had no complications and is now one month from her operation. Her pathology did not reveal any malignancies. I hope this video was helpful. As always, thank you again for watching.
